info.parenttitle: 3.2 Variations in economic activity - aggregate demand and aggregate supply

markdown:
1. The **Price Level** (**PL**) of an economy** is not the only** **factor** that **affects** its **aggregate demand** (**AD**). 
2. While changes in the **PL** cause **movements** along the **AD curve**, changes in the **determinants of the AD components** (C+I+G+(X-M)) **shift** the **AD curve**.

<definition term="Determinants of aggregate demand">Factors that **shift** the **aggregate demand curve** by affecting its **components**: consumption (C), investment (I), government spending (G), and net exports (X - M).</definition>

The **determinants of AD components** covered in the IB curriculum are:

1. **Consumer spending (C) determinants**:
  1. Consumer confidence.
  2. Interest rates.
  3. Wealth.
  4. Income taxes.
  5. Level of household indebtedness.
  6. Expectations of future price level.
2. **Investment (I) determinants:**
  1. Interest rates.
  2. Business confidence.
  3. Technology.
  4. Business taxes.
  5. Level of corporate indebtedness.
3. **Government spending (G) determinants:**
  1. Political and economic priorities.
4. **Net exports (X-M) determinants:**
  1. Income of trading partners.
  2. Exchange rates.
  3. Trade policies.

<callout type="note">The most important thing to note in this topic is that:

1. Changes in **price level** cause changes the quantity of **real GDP** demanded   (**causing a movement along the AD curve**).
2. Changes in the **determinants of AD components** lead to changes in the quantity of **real GDP** demanded at **all possible price levels** (**causing a shift of the demand curve**).
  1. A **rightward shift** indicates that at all given price levels, the total quantity of **real GDP demanded** **increases**.
  2. A **leftward shift** indicates that at all given price levels, the total quantity of **real GDP demanded decreases**.

![Figure 1: movements versus shifts along the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/a82b56a7cf017e41bed4c10ec78cbab764b05402-2399x980.png)</callout>

<callout type="exam_technique">In IB Economics long-answer questions, it is very important to explain processes step-by-step, without skipping any steps.

In this section, the effect of changes in the determinants of AD components will be explained in such way.</callout>

## Consumer spending (C) determinants


### Consumer confidence


#### Increased consumer confidence → rightward shift in AD


1. **Consumer** **confidence** refers to the level of **optimism** consumers feel about the **economy's** **overall** **condition** and their **personal** **financial** situation.
2. If an **event** causes **consumers** to feel **more** **confident** about the economy's condition or their personal financial situation, **consumer** **confidence** **increases**.
3. **Increased** **consumer** **confidence** causes **consumers** to **increase** **their** **spending**, since they are confident in the economic conditions.
4. Since **consumer** **spending** is one of the **components** **of** **AD**, higher consumer spending leads to **higher** **AD** **at** **all** **price** **levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **increase** in **consumer** **confidence** **shifts** the **AD** **curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2). 

![Figure 2: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased consumer confidence → leftward shift in AD


1. **Consumer confidence** refers to the level of optimism consumers feel about the economy's overall condition and their personal financial situation.
2. If an **event** causes consumers to feel **less** **confident** about the economy's condition or their personal financial situation, **consumer confidence decreases.**
3. **Decreased consumer confidence** causes consumers to **reduce** **their** **spending**, as they feel uncertain or pessimistic about the economic conditions and want to retain their financial resources.
4. Since **consumer spending** is one of the **components** **of** **AD**, lower consumer spending leads to **lower** **AD** **at** **all** **price** **levels**.
5. Resultantly, a decrease in **consumer confidence** shifts the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 3: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

### Interest rates


<definition term="Interest rates">Interest stated as a percentage. It represents the proportion of the amount of money borrowed charged as interest.</definition>

#### Increased interest rates → leftward shift in AD


1. **Interest rates** refer to the **cost of borrowing money** or the **reward for saving**.
2. If **interest rates rise**, **borrowing** becomes **more** **expensive** for consumers, while the **incentive to save increases**.
3. Hence, **increased interest rates** discourage consumers from spending, as they have a higher incentive to save.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of AD**, **lower spending** leads to **lower** **AD** **at all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **increase in interest rates** shifts the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 4: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased interest rates → rightward shift in AD


1. **Interest rates** refer to the **cost of borrowing money** or the **reward for saving**.
2. If **interest rates fall**, **borrowing** becomes **cheaper** for consumers, while the **incentive** **to** **save** **decreases**.
3. Hence, **decreased interest rates** encourage consumers to **spend**, as the as the cost of financing is lower.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of AD**, **higher spending** leads to **higher** **AD** **at all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **decrease in interest rates** shifts the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 5: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

### Wealth


#### Increased wealth → rightward shift in AD


1. **Wealth** refers to the total value of a household’s assets, such as property, stocks, and savings, minus liabilities.
2. When **wealth increases**, consumers feel more financially secure and confident about their economic situation.
3. This encourages **higher consumer spending**, as households are willing to use their **additional wealth** to buy goods and services.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of AD**, **increased spending** leads to **higher** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **increase in wealth** shifts the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 6: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased wealth → leftward shift in AD


1. **Wealth** refers to the total value of a household’s assets, such as property, stocks, and savings, minus liabilities.
2. When **wealth decreases**, consumers feel less financially secure and more uncertain about their economic situation.
3. This **discourages consumer spending**, as households may **cut back on expenditures** to compensate for the **loss in wealth**.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of AD**, **reduced spending** leads to **lower** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, a **decrease in wealth** shifts the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 7: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

### Income taxes


<definition term="Income taxes">Taxes paid by households on their incomes.</definition>

#### Increased income taxes → leftward shift in AD


1. **Income taxes** refer to taxes imposed by the government on households' incomes, which reduce disposable income.
2. When **income taxes increase**, consumers have **less** **disposable** **income** to spend on goods and services.
3. This **discourages consumer spending**, as households need to allocate more of their income to tax payments and **less to consumption**.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of** **AD**, **lower spending** leads to **lower** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **increase in income taxes** shifts the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 8: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased income taxes → rightward shift in AD


1. **Income taxes** refer to taxes imposed by the government on households' incomes, which reduce disposable income.
2. When **income taxes decrease**, consumers have **more** **disposable** **income** to spend on goods and services.
3. This encourages **higher** **consumer** **spending**, as households retain (and can spend) a greater share of their income.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component** **of** **AD**, **higher** **spending** leads to **higher** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, a **decrease in income taxes** shifts the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 9: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

### Level of household indebtedness


#### Increased household indebtedness → leftward shift in AD


1. **Household indebtedness** refers to the total amount of debt that households owe, such as mortgages, credit card debt, and loans.
2. When an even causes **household indebtedness **to **increase**, consumers allocate **more** of their income toward **repaying** **debt** and **less** of it on **spending** on goods and services.
3. This **reduces** **consumer** **spending**, as households **prioritise** **debt** **repayments** over consumption.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of AD**, **lower spending** leads to **lower** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **increase in household indebtedness** shifts the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 10: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased household indebtedness → rightward shift in AD


1. **Household indebtedness** refers to the total amount of debt that households owe, such as mortgages, credit card debt, and loans.
2. When an even causes **household indebtedness **to **decrease**, consumers allocate **less** of their income toward **repaying** **debt**, leaving **more** income available for **spending**.
3. This **increases** **consumer** **spending**, as households feel less constrained by debt obligations.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of AD**, **higher spending** leads to **higher** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **decrease in household indebtedness** shifts the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 11: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

### Expectations of future price level


#### Expectations of higher future prices → rightward shift in AD


1. **Expectations of future price levels** refer to what consumers anticipate about how prices will change in the future.
2. If consumers expect **prices** **to** **rise** in the future, they are likely to **increase their spending now** to avoid paying higher prices later.
3. This causes an **increase** in current **consumer spending**, which is a **component** **of** **AD**.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of AD**, **higher spending** leads to **higher** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **expectations of higher future prices** shift the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 12: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

#### Expectations of lower future prices → leftward shift in AD


1. **Expectations of future price levels** refer to what consumers anticipate about how prices will change in the future.
2. If consumers expect **prices** **to** **fall** in the future, they are likely to **decrease their spending now** hoping to pay lower prices later.
3. This causes a** decrease** in current **consumer spending**, which is a **component** **of** **AD**.
4. Since **consumer spending** is a **component of AD**, **lower spending** leads to **lower** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **expectations of lower future prices** shift the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 13: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

## Investment (I) determinants


### Interest rates


<definition term="Interest rates">Interest stated as a percentage. It represents the proportion of the amount of money borrowed charged as interest.</definition>

#### Increased interest rates → leftward shift in AD


1. **Interest rates** refer to the **cost of borrowing money** or the **reward for saving**.
2. If **interest rates rise**, **borrowing** becomes **more** **expensive** for business. 
3. Hence, **increased interest rates** make **investing** in new projects or capital equipment **less** **attractive** for firms. Additionally, **higher** **interest** **rates** encourage businesses to **save** **rather** than **spend **on **investments**.
4. Since **firms investment** is a **component of AD**, **lower investment** leads to **lower** **AD** at** all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **increase in interest rates** shifts the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 14: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased interest rates → rightward shift in AD


1. **Interest rates** refer to the **cost of borrowing money** or the **reward for saving**.
2. If **interest rates fall**, **borrowing** becomes **cheaper** for business. 
3. Hence, **decreased interest rates** make **investing** in new projects or capital equipment **more** **attractive** for firms. Additionally, **lower** **interest** **rates** reduce the incentive to **save**, making **spending **on **investments** more attractive for businesses.
4. Since **firms investment** is a **component of AD**, **higher investment** leads to **higher** **AD** at** all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, a **decrease in interest rates** shifts the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 15: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

### Business confidence


#### Increased business confidence → rightward shift in AD


1. **Business confidence** refers to the level of optimism that firms have about the economy's future performance and profitability.
2. If an **event** causes **businesses** to feel **more** **optimistic** about future economic conditions, **business** **confidence** **increases**.
3. **Increased** **businesses** **confidence** causes **businesses** to **increase** **their** **investments **in new projects, equipment, and expansion, since they are confident about future profitability.
4. Since **investment** is one of the **components** **of** **AD**, **higher** firm **investment** **spending **leads to **higher** **AD** at **all** **price** **levels**.
5. Resultantly, an **increase** in **businesses** **confidence** **shifts** the **AD** **curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2). 

![Figure 16: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased business confidence → leftward shift in AD


1. **Business confidence** refers to the level of optimism that firms have about the economy's future performance and profitability.
2. If an **event** causes **businesses** to feel **less** **optimistic** about future economic conditions, **business** **confidence** **decreases**.
3. **Decreased** **businesses** **confidence** causes **businesses** to **decrease** **their** **investments **in new projects, equipment, and expansion, since they are less confident about future profitability.
4. Since **investment** is one of the **components** **of** **AD**, **lower** firm **investment spending** leads to **lower** **AD** at **all** **price** **levels**.
5. Resultantly, a **decrease** in **businesses** **confidence** **shifts** the **AD** **curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2). 

![Figure 17: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

### Technology


#### Technological advancements → rightward shift in AD


1. **Technological advancement** refers to the development and application of innovations that improve **productivity** and **efficiency** in production processes.
2. If **technological advancements** occur, firms can produce goods and services more **efficiently**, reducing **production** **costs** and increasing profitability.
3. This encourages firms to **increase investment spending** in adopting and implementing new technology.
4. Since **investment** is a component of **AD**, **higher investment spending** leads to **higher** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **an improvement in technology** **shifts** the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 18: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

#### Technological stagnation → leftward shift in AD


1. **Technological advancement** refers to the development and application of innovations that improve **productivity** and **efficiency** in production processes.
2. If **technological stagnation** occurs, where there is little to no advancement in technology, firms may experience **higher** **production** **costs** and reduced productivity.
3. This discourages firms from **investing** in new projects or equipment, as the **lack** **of** **innovation** **limits** potential **profitability**.
4. Since **investment** is a component of **AD**, **lower** **investment spending** leads to **lower** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **a lack of technological progress** **shifts** the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 19: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

### Business taxes


#### Higher business taxes → leftward shift in AD


1. **Business taxes** refer to taxes imposed on firms' income, profits, or production by the government.
2. If **business taxes increase**, firms face **higher** **operational** **costs**, since a greater part of their revenue needs to be allocated to paying the business taxes. 
3. This **discourages** firms from **spending on new investments**, as higher taxes lower profitability.
4. Since **investment** is a **component of** **AD**, **lower** **investment spending** leads to **lower** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **an increase in business taxes** **shifts** the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 20: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

#### Lower business taxes → rightward shift in AD


1. **Business taxes** refer to taxes imposed on firms' income, profits, or production by the government.
2. If **business taxes decrease**, firms face **lower** **operational** **costs**, since a smaller part of their revenue needs to be allocated to paying the business taxes. 
3. This encourages firms to allocate their spare revenue to **increase their investment spending** in equipment, infrastructure, or other productive ventures.
4. Since **investment** is a **component of** **AD**, **higher** **investment spending** leads to **higher** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **a reduction in business taxes** shifts the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 21: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

### Level of corporate indebtedness


#### Higher corporate indebtedness → leftward shift in AD


1. **Corporate indebtedness** refers to the level of debt that firms have accumulated through borrowing.
2. If **corporate indebtedness is high**, firms must allocate a larger portion of their resources toward debt repayment, reducing funds available for **new investments**.
3. This discourages firms from **spending on investments**, as they focus on managing their existing financial obligations.
4. Since **investment** is a **component of** **AD**, **lower** **investment spending** leads to **lower** **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **a higher level of corporate indebtedness** **shifts** the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 22: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

#### Lower corporate indebtedness → rightward shift in AD


1. **Corporate indebtedness** refers to the level of debt that firms have accumulated through borrowing.
2. If **corporate indebtedness is low**, firms have fewer financial obligations to repay, leaving them with more resources to invest in new projects, equipment, or expansion.
3. This encourages firms to **increase their investment spending**, as they are in a better financial position to take on new ventures.
4. Since **investment** is a **component of AD**, **higher** **investment spending** leads to higher **AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **a lower level of corporate indebtedness** **shifts** the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 23: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

## Government spending (G) determinants


### Political and economic priorities


#### Increased government spending due to political/economic priorities → rightward shift in AD


1. **Government spending (G)** refers to expenditures by the government on goods and services, including infrastructure, education, and healthcare.
2. When **political or economic priorities** shift toward **increased government spending** (funding public projects, social programs, or economic stimulus packages...),** government expenditure **rises.
3. Since **government spending (G) **is a **component of AD**, increased in **government spending** leads to **higher** **aggregate demand (AD)** at all price levels.
4. Resultantly, **increased government spending due to political or economic priorities** **shifts** the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 24: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased government spending due to political/economic priorities → leftward shift in AD


1. **Government spending (G)** refers to expenditures by the government on goods and services, including infrastructure, education, and healthcare.
2. When **political or economic priorities** shift toward **decreased government spending** (implementing austerity measures, ideological reasons...),** government expenditure **falls.
3. Since **government spending (G) **is a **component of AD**, a decrease in **government spending** leads to **lower** **aggregate demand (AD)** at all price levels.
4. Resultantly, **decreased government spending due to political or economic priorities** **shifts** the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 25: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

## Net exports (X-M) determinants


### Income of trading partners


#### Increased income of trading partners → rightward shift in AD


1. The **income of trading partners** refers to the income of the countries that **import** goods and services from the domestic economy.
2. When the **income** of trading partners **increases**, their ability to **purchase** **imports** from the domestic economy **rises**.
3. This leads to **higher** **exports (X)** for the domestic economy, which **increases** **net exports (X - M)** as imports are assumed to remain unchanged.
4. Since **net exports** are a component of **aggregate demand (AD)**, **higher** **net** **exports** result in an **increase** **in AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **an increase in the income of trading partners** shifts the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 26: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

#### Decreased income of trading partners → leftward shift in AD


1. The **income of trading partners** refers to the income of the countries that **import** goods and services from the domestic economy.
2. When the **income** of trading partners **decreases**, their ability to **purchase** **imports** from the domestic economy **falls**.
3. This leads to **lower** **exports (X)** for the domestic economy, which **decreases** **net exports (X - M)** as imports are assumed to remain unchanged.
4. Since **net exports** are a component of **aggregate demand (AD)**, **lower** **net** **exports** result in a **decrease** **in AD** at **all price levels**.
5. Resultantly, **a decrease in the income of trading partners** shifts the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 27: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

### Exchange rates


<definition term="Exchange Rate">An **exchange rate** is the **value** of one **currency** in terms of another **currency**.</definition>

#### Appreciation of the domestic currency → leftward shift in AD


1. **Exchange rates** refer to the value of one currency in terms of another.
2. When the **domestic currency appreciates**, the same amount of domestic is equivalent to a greater amount of the foreign currency.
3. Therefore, imports become cheaper, encouraging their purchase by **domestic** **consumers**. At the same time, the price of domestic exports for **foreigners** **increases**, making them more expensive and decreasing their demand.
4. As a result, **exports (X)** **decrease** and **imports (M)** **increase**, leading to a **decrease** in **net exports (X - M)**.
5. Since **net exports** are a component of **aggregate demand (AD)**, **lower** **net** **exports** result in a **decrease** **in AD** at **all price levels**.
6. Resultantly, **an appreciation of the domestic currency** shifts the **AD curve** to the **left** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 28: leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/b6f5910e8d9c6b8d6eb0e2bac78fd15237ac498b-2400x1056.png)

#### Depreciation of the domestic currency → rightward shift in AD


1. **Exchange rates** refer to the value of one currency in terms of another.
2. When the **domestic currency depreciates**, the same amount of domestic is equivalent to a lesser amount of the foreign currency.
3. Therefore, imports become more expensive, discouraging their purchase by **domestic** **consumers**. At the same time, the price of domestic exports for **foreigners** **decreases**, making them cheaper and increasing their demand.
4. As a result, **exports (X)** **increase** and **imports (M)** **decrease**, leading to an **increase** in **net exports (X - M)**.
5. Since **net exports** are a component of **aggregate demand (AD)**, **higher** **net** **exports** result in an **increase** **in AD** at **all price levels**.
6. Resultantly, **a depreciation of the domestic currency** shifts the **AD curve** to the **right** (AD1→AD2).

![Figure 29: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

<callout type="warning">It seems unintuitive that a currency appreciation leads to a fall in AD, so many students often mistake this concept in the exam.</callout>

### Trade policies


#### Trade liberalisation → rightward shift in AD


1. **Trade policies** refer to government regulations that influence international trade, such as tariffs, quotas, or free trade agreements.
2. When a country **reduces trade barriers** (lowers tariffs, removes import quotas...), it encourages **greater trade activity**.
3. Greater trade activity may alter the **net exports (X-M) **positively or negatively, depending on the measures take by the government.
  1. If **(X-M) increases**, since **net exports** are a component of **aggregate demand (AD)**, **increased** **net** **exports** result in a **higher** **AD** at all **price levels**. This causes a **shift** to the **right** of the **AD curve**.
  2. If **(X-M) decreases**, since **net exports** are a component of **aggregate demand (AD)**, **decreased** **net** **exports** result in a **lower** **AD** at all **price levels**. This causes a **shift** to the **left** of the **AD curve**.

![Figure 30: rightward shift of the AD curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5e3e2f17ecad83623e424b5265dbc5d9d99d1c1c-2400x1056.png)

<callout type="note">We will learn how different trade regulations can affect the **net exports (X-M) **balance in _Chapter 4_.</callout>

<callout type="self_review">Go over all the **determinants of AD components** and try to explain their effect on the **AD** and the **AD curve** step-by-step.

If you master this, Paper 1 will become much easier!</callout>

nexttopic.title: 3.2.4 Shifts of the AD curve caused by changes in determinants
